collections.title: My Picks

collections.title: Docs & Research

skills.name: frontend-design
skills.description: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use for building web components, pages, dashboards, HTML/CSS layouts, or styling any web UI.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's frontend design guidance is still high-signal for interface craft, visual direction, and polished product execution without collapsing into generic app UI.

skills.name: pdf
skills.description: Use when the task involves reading, extracting from, merging, splitting, or generating PDF files.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's PDF guidance is still high-signal for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Spreadsheets guidance is still high-signal for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: docx

skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Documents guidance is still high-signal for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Presentations guidance is still high-signal for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's MCP guidance is still high-signal for systems, architecture, and deeper codebase work, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Skills guidance is still high-signal for execution, planning, and day-to-day operating flow, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: doc-coauthoring
skills.description: Use when drafting, revising, or coauthoring long-form docs, specs, or written deliverables with the user.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Writing skill remains one of the strongest starting points for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output.

skills.name: canvas-design
skills.description: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. Use for creating posters, art, designs, or other static visuals.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Canvas guidance is still high-signal for visual direction and design craft, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: algorithmic-art
skills.description: Creating algorithmic art using p5.js with seeded randomness and interactive parameter exploration. Use for generative art, flow fields, or particle systems.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Generative Art guidance is still high-signal for visual direction and design craft, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: webapp-testing
skills.description: Use when QAing a web app, checking regressions, or turning manual test flows into concrete repros.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Web QA guidance is still high-signal for review, QA, and product-quality work, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: brand-guidelines
skills.description: Applies official brand colors, typography, and styling to artifacts. Use for creating branded content, marketing materials, or maintaining design consistency.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Brand guidance is still high-signal for adjacent business and operating work, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: internal-comms
skills.description: Write internal communications using company formats. Use for status reports, leadership updates, newsletters, FAQs, incident reports, or project updates.
skills.author: anthropics
skills.whyHere: Kept because Anthropic's Communication guidance is still high-signal for adjacent business and operating work, and the library ships a stable snapshot so installs stay deterministic.

skills.name: backend-development
skills.description: Backend API design, database architecture, microservices patterns, and test-driven development. Use for designing APIs, database schemas, or backend systems.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Covers API design patterns, service architecture, and error handling at the backend layer. Fills a gap that frontend-focused skill sets leave open.

skills.name: database-design
skills.description: Database schema design, optimization, and migration patterns for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and NoSQL databases. Use for designing schemas or optimizing queries.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Gives agents real schema design guidance: normalization, indexing strategy, migration patterns. Most general skills treat the database as an afterthought.

skills.name: llm-application-dev
skills.description: Building applications with Large Language Models - prompt engineering, RAG patterns, and LLM integration. Use for AI-powered features or chatbots.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Covers prompt engineering, token management, and LLM integration patterns from a backend perspective. Relevant whenever the codebase talks to a language model.

skills.name: code-documentation
skills.description: Writing effective code documentation - API docs, README files, inline comments, and technical guides. Use for documenting codebases or APIs.
skills.author: wshobson
skills.whyHere: Kept because this wshobson pattern still earns a place for docs, file-heavy tasks, and long-form output, even though upstream has since reorganized around plugins.

skills.name: job-application
skills.description: Use when tailoring resumes, writing applications, or preparing role-specific job materials.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Original library skill for structured application writing, tailoring, and career positioning work.

skills.name: ask-questions-if-underspecified
skills.description: Clarify requirements before implementing. Ask 1-5 must-have questions to avoid wrong work. Use when requests are ambiguous, have multiple valid interpretations, or lack key details like scope, constraints, or acceptance criteria.
skills.author: thsottiaux
skills.whyHere: Kept because this requirement-clarification pattern is one of the cleanest ways to stop agents from running ahead on underspecified work.

skills.name: best-practices
skills.description: Transform vague prompts into optimized Claude Code instructions. Adds verification, context, constraints, and proper phasing. Use when prompts lack test cases, specific locations, or success criteria.
skills.author: MoizIbnYousaf
skills.whyHere: Original library skill that sets the quality bar for prompts, constraints, and implementation workflow across the library.

skills.name: changelog-generator
skills.description: Use when turning commits, shipped work, or release notes into a readable changelog.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Kept because release notes are a real recurring workflow task, and this one is more practical than a generic writing skill when a ship checklist needs finishing.

skills.name: content-research-writer
skills.description: Use when researching a topic and turning the findings into a usable written brief or draft.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Kept because the research shelf needs one skill that turns raw findings into a usable written brief instead of stopping at collection.

skills.name: lead-research-assistant
skills.description: Use when researching leads, accounts, or prospects before outreach or prioritization.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Kept because it adds targeted lead and account research to the shelf, which is a distinct job from general content research.

skills.name: video-downloader
skills.description: Use when a task needs source video for editing, reference, clipping, or offline review.
skills.author: composio
skills.whyHere: Kept because it still earns a place for visual direction and design craft, but the library ships a stable snapshot instead of tracking every upstream change.

skills.name: openai-docs
skills.description: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation, current model guidance, or GPT-5.4 upgrade help.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because it gives the library a current, official path into OpenAI docs, model guidance, and implementation help.

skills.name: gh-fix-ci
skills.description: Use when GitHub Actions CI is failing and the task is to diagnose or fix the pipeline.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because OpenAI's CI skill is current, high-signal, and fits the library well for review, QA, and product-quality work.

skills.name: figma
skills.description: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, then translate nodes into production code.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because OpenAI's Figma skill is current, high-signal, and fits the library well for visual direction and design craft.

skills.name: sentry
skills.description: Use when investigating production errors, tracing issues, or working from Sentry events.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because OpenAI's Observability skill is current, high-signal, and fits the library well for review, QA, and product-quality work.

skills.name: playwright
skills.description: Use when browser automation, end-to-end testing, or reproducing UI behavior in a real browser is required.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because OpenAI's Browser Automation skill is current, high-signal, and fits the library well for review, QA, and product-quality work.

skills.name: linear
skills.description: Manage issues, projects & team workflows in Linear. Use when the user wants to read, create or updates tickets in Linear.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because it gives the workflow shelf a real project-management endpoint instead of only prompting advice, and Linear is a tool I actually reach for.

skills.name: frontend-skill
skills.description: Use when the task asks for a visually strong landing page, website, app, prototype, demo, or game UI. This skill enforces restrained composition, image-led hierarchy, cohesive content structure, and tasteful motion while avoiding generic cards, weak branding, and UI clutter.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because OpenAI's frontend taste is distinct from Anthropic's interface-craft guidance; together they make the frontend shelf feel like a real set instead of one repo repeated.

skills.name: shadcn
skills.description: Manages shadcn components and projects — adding, searching, fixing, debugging, styling, and composing UI. Provides project context, component docs, and usage examples. Applies when working with shadcn/ui, component registries, presets, --preset codes, or any project with a components.json file. Also triggers for "shadcn init", "create an app with --preset", or "switch to --preset".
skills.author: shadcn-ui
skills.whyHere: Kept because shadcn/ui is a real part of modern frontend work, and this skill gives the shelf practical component-system leverage instead of only visual theory.

skills.name: emil-design-eng
skills.description: Use when the task needs sharper UI polish, motion judgment, spacing, or design-engineering craft.
skills.author: emilkowalski
skills.whyHere: Kept because Emil's sensibility sharpens polish, spacing, motion, and restraint in a way the rest of the shelf does not.

skills.name: security-best-practices
skills.description: Perform language and framework specific security best-practice reviews and suggest improvements. Trigger only when the user explicitly requests security best practices guidance, a security review/report, or secure-by-default coding help. Trigger only for supported languages (python, javascript/typescript, go). Do not trigger for general code review, debugging, or non-security tasks.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because the backend shelf needed an explicit security lens, not just architecture and data-modeling guidance.

skills.name: notion-spec-to-implementation
skills.description: Turn Notion specs into implementation plans, tasks, and progress tracking; use when implementing PRDs/feature specs and creating Notion plans + tasks from them.
skills.author: openai
skills.whyHere: Kept because the workflow shelf should include a real spec-to-plan bridge, not just ticketing and prompting.

skills.name: userinterface-wiki
skills.description: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
skills.author: raphaelsalaja
skills.whyHere: High-signal UI/UX review skill for interface polish, implementation critique, and stronger frontend craft.

skills.name: swiftui-pro
skills.description: Comprehensively reviews SwiftUI code for best practices on modern APIs, maintainability, and performance. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing SwiftUI projects.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Curated because the SwiftUI branch needs concrete build and refactor guidance from installable upstream skills, not just a list of links.

skills.name: swiftui-ui-patterns
skills.description: Best practices and example-driven guidance for building SwiftUI views and components, including navigation hierarchies, custom view modifiers, and responsive layouts with stacks and grids. Use when creating or refactoring SwiftUI UI, designing tab architecture with TabView, composing screens with VStack/HStack, managing @State or @Binding, building declarative iOS interfaces, or needing component-specific patterns and examples.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Curated because the SwiftUI branch needs concrete build and refactor guidance from installable upstream skills, not just a list of links.

skills.name: swiftui-design-principles
skills.description: Design principles for building polished, native-feeling SwiftUI apps and widgets. Use this skill when creating or modifying SwiftUI views, iOS widgets (WidgetKit), or any native Apple UI. Ensures proper spacing, typography, colors, and widget implementations that look and feel like quality apps rather than AI-generated slop.
skills.author: arjitj2
skills.whyHere: Curated because the SwiftUI branch needs concrete build and refactor guidance from installable upstream skills, not just a list of links.

skills.name: swiftui-view-refactor
skills.description: Refactor and review SwiftUI view files with strong defaults for small dedicated subviews, MV-over-MVVM data flow, stable view trees, explicit dependency injection, and correct Observation usage. Use when cleaning up a SwiftUI view, splitting long bodies, removing inline actions or side effects, reducing computed `some View` helpers, or standardizing `@Observable` and view model initialization patterns.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Curated because the SwiftUI branch needs concrete build and refactor guidance from installable upstream skills, not just a list of links.

skills.name: swiftdata-pro
skills.description: Writes, reviews, and improves SwiftData code using modern APIs and best practices. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing projects that use SwiftData.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Curated because SwiftData work benefits from specialist skills that stay upstream and keep pace with Apple framework changes.

skills.name: swiftdata-expert-skill
skills.description: Expert guidance for designing, implementing, migrating, and debugging SwiftData persistence in Swift and SwiftUI apps. Use when working with @Model schemas, @Relationship/@Attribute rules, Query or FetchDescriptor data access, ModelContainer/ModelContext configuration, CloudKit sync, SchemaMigrationPlan/history APIs, ModelActor concurrency isolation, or Core Data to SwiftData adoption/coexistence.
skills.author: vanab
skills.whyHere: Curated because SwiftData work benefits from specialist skills that stay upstream and keep pace with Apple framework changes.

skills.name: swift-concurrency-pro
skills.description: Reviews Swift code for concurrency correctness, modern API usage, and common async/await pitfalls. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing Swift concurrency code.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Curated because concurrency is one of the sharpest failure modes in modern Swift, and this branch needs real review and remediation skills.

skills.name: swift-concurrency-expert
skills.description: Swift Concurrency review and remediation for Swift 6.2+. Use when asked to review Swift Concurrency usage, improve concurrency compliance, or fix Swift concurrency compiler errors in a feature or file. Concrete actions include adding Sendable conformance, applying @MainActor annotations, resolving actor isolation warnings, fixing data race diagnostics, and migrating completion handlers to async/await.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Curated because concurrency is one of the sharpest failure modes in modern Swift, and this branch needs real review and remediation skills.

skills.name: swift-concurrency
skills.description: Diagnose data races, convert callback-based code to async/await, implement actor isolation patterns, resolve Sendable conformance issues, and guide Swift 6 migration. Use when developers mention: (1) Swift Concurrency, async/await, actors, or tasks, (2) "use Swift Concurrency" or "modern concurrency patterns", (3) migrating to Swift 6, (4) data races or thread safety issues, (5) refactoring closures to async/await, (6) @MainActor, Sendable, or actor isolation, (7) concurrent code architecture or performance optimization, (8) concurrency-related linter warnings (SwiftLint or similar; e.g. async_without_await, Sendable/actor isolation/MainActor lint).
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Curated because concurrency is one of the sharpest failure modes in modern Swift, and this branch needs real review and remediation skills.

skills.name: swift-testing-pro
skills.description: Writes, reviews, and improves Swift Testing code using modern APIs and best practices. Use when reading, writing, or reviewing projects that use Swift Testing.
skills.author: twostraws
skills.whyHere: Curated because Swift testing work needs installable specialists for migration, review, and execution, not another reading list.

skills.name: swift-testing
skills.description: Expert guidance on Swift Testing best practices, patterns, and implementation. Use when developers mention: (1) Swift Testing, @Test, #expect, #require, or @Suite, (2) "use Swift Testing" or "modern testing patterns", (3) test doubles, mocks, stubs, spies, or fixtures, (4) unit tests, integration tests, or snapshot tests, (5) migrating from XCTest to Swift Testing, (6) TDD, Arrange-Act-Assert, or F.I.R.S.T. principles, (7) parameterized tests or test organization.
skills.author: bocato
skills.whyHere: Curated because Swift testing work needs installable specialists for migration, review, and execution, not another reading list.

skills.name: swift-testing-expert
skills.description: Expert guidance for Swift Testing: test structure, #expect/#require macros, traits and tags, parameterized tests, test plans, parallel execution, async waiting patterns, and XCTest migration. Use when writing new Swift tests, modernizing XCTest suites, debugging flaky tests, or improving test quality and maintainability in Apple-platform or Swift server projects.
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Curated because Swift testing work needs installable specialists for migration, review, and execution, not another reading list.

skills.name: swift-api-design-guidelines-skill
skills.description: Write, review, or improve Swift APIs using Swift API Design Guidelines for naming, argument labels, documentation comments, terminology, and general conventions. Use when designing new APIs, refactoring existing interfaces, or reviewing API clarity and fluency.
skills.author: Erikote04
skills.whyHere: Curated because API and language discipline shape the whole Swift codebase, so the mobile shelf should expose a real skill here.

skills.name: ios-accessibility
skills.description: Expert guidance on iOS accessibility best practices, patterns, and implementation. Use when developers mention: (1) iOS accessibility, VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, or assistive technologies, (2) accessibility labels, traits, hints, or values, (3) automated accessibility testing, auditing, or manual testing, (4) Switch Control, Voice Control, or Full Keyboard Access, (5) inclusive design or accessibility culture, (6) making apps work for users with disabilities.
skills.author: dadederk
skills.whyHere: Curated because accessibility deserves first-class mobile coverage with concrete Apple-platform audits and remediation guidance.

skills.name: swift-accessibility-skill
skills.description: Apply platform accessibility best practices to SwiftUI, UIKit, and AppKit code. Essential companion to any SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit skill — always use together. Use whenever writing, editing, or reviewing ANY SwiftUI views, UIKit view controllers, AppKit views/window controllers, or platform UI — even when the user doesn't mention accessibility. Also use when the user mentions VoiceOver, Voice Control, Dynamic Type, Reduce Motion, screen reader, a11y, WCAG, accessibility audit, Nutrition Labels, accessibilityLabel, UIAccessibility, NSAccessibility, assistive technologies, or Switch Control. Not for server-side Swift, non-UI packages, or CLI tools.
skills.author: PasqualeVittoriosi
skills.whyHere: Curated because accessibility deserves first-class mobile coverage with concrete Apple-platform audits and remediation guidance.

skills.name: appkit-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Audit macOS AppKit interfaces for accessibility, focusing on VoiceOver, keyboard navigation, and semantics
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: Curated because accessibility deserves first-class mobile coverage with concrete Apple-platform audits and remediation guidance.

skills.name: swiftui-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Audit SwiftUI views for accessibility (iOS + macOS) with patch-ready fixes
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: Curated because accessibility deserves first-class mobile coverage with concrete Apple-platform audits and remediation guidance.

skills.name: uikit-accessibility-auditor
skills.description: Audit UIKit-based screens for accessibility issues with concrete VoiceOver and Dynamic Type fixes
skills.author: rgmez
skills.whyHere: Curated because accessibility deserves first-class mobile coverage with concrete Apple-platform audits and remediation guidance.

skills.name: swift-architecture-skill
skills.description: Swift architecture patterns and playbooks for MVVM, TCA, Clean Architecture, and more.
skills.author: efremidze
skills.whyHere: Curated because Swift architecture choices compound quickly, and this branch benefits from a dedicated upstream opinionated skill.

skills.name: core-data-expert
skills.description: Expert Core Data guidance (iOS/macOS): stack setup, fetch requests & NSFetchedResultsController, saving/merge conflicts, threading & Swift Concurrency, batch operations & persistent history, migrations, performance, and NSPersistentCloudKitContainer/CloudKit sync.
skills.author: AvdLee
skills.whyHere: Curated because legacy Apple persistence still matters in real apps, and the mobile shelf needs a direct Core Data specialist.

skills.name: swiftui-performance-audit
skills.description: Audit and improve SwiftUI runtime performance from code review and architecture. Use for requests to diagnose slow rendering, janky scrolling, high CPU/memory usage, excessive view updates, or layout thrash in SwiftUI apps, and to provide guidance for user-run Instruments profiling when code review alone is insufficient.
skills.author: Dimillian
skills.whyHere: Curated because SwiftUI performance problems are subtle, and this branch needs a focused audit skill with real implementation guidance.

skills.name: swift-security-expert
skills.description: Use when working with iOS/macOS Keychain Services (SecItem queries, kSecClass, OSStatus errors), biometric authentication (LAContext, Face ID, Touch ID), CryptoKit (AES-GCM, ChaChaPoly, ECDSA, ECDH, HPKE, ML-KEM), Secure Enclave, secure credential storage (OAuth tokens, API keys), certificate pinning (SecTrust, SPKI), keychain sharing across apps/extensions, migrating secrets from UserDefaults or plists, or OWASP MASVS/MASTG mobile compliance on Apple platforms.
skills.author: ivan-magda
skills.whyHere: Curated because security work should be native to the mobile shelf, with a real Swift-focused specialist instead of generic advice.

skills.name: ios-simulator-skill
skills.description: 21 production-ready scripts for iOS app testing, building, and automation. Provides semantic UI navigation, build automation, accessibility testing, and simulator lifecycle management. Optimized for AI agents with minimal token output.
skills.author: conorluddy
skills.whyHere: Curated because practical simulator and device workflows belong in the mobile shelf when agents need to operate Swift apps end to end.

skills.name: writing-for-interfaces
skills.description: Use when someone asks to write, rewrite, review, or improve text that appears inside a product or interface. Examples: "review the UX copy", "is there a better way to phrase this", "rewrite this error message", "write copy for this screen/flow/page", reviewing button labels, improving CLI output messages, writing onboarding copy, settings descriptions, or confirmation dialogs. Trigger whenever the request involves wording shown to end users inside software — apps, web, CLI, email notifications, modals, tooltips, empty states, or alerts. Also trigger for vague requests like "review the UX" where interface copy review is implied. Do NOT trigger for content marketing, blog posts, app store listings, API docs, brand guides, cover letters, or interview questions — this is a technical writing skill for interface language.
skills.author: andrewgleave
skills.whyHere: Curated because interface writing is part of product craft on Apple platforms, and the mobile shelf should include that discipline too.
